text: The LORD appeared to Abraham by the oaks of Mamre as he sat at his tent door in the heat of the day.
Abraham looked up and saw three men standing near him; he ran to meet them and bowed to the ground.
He asked them, if he had found favor, please not to pass by their servant.
He offered to bring water to wash their feet, and for them to rest under the tree.
He offered to bring bread so they could refresh themselves before going on; they agreed.
Abraham hurried to Sarah in the tent and told her to quickly prepare fine flour into cakes.
He ran to the herd, chose a tender calf, and gave it to a servant to prepare quickly.
He took curds, milk, and the calf, and set it before them, standing by as they ate under the tree.
They asked where Sarah his wife was; he said she was in the tent.
The LORD said he would return about this time next year, and Sarah would have a son; Sarah was listening at the tent door behind him.
Abraham and Sarah were old, well along in years; Sarah had passed the age of childbearing.
So Sarah laughed to herself, saying, after I am worn out and my husband is old, shall I have pleasure?
The LORD asked Abraham why Sarah laughed, doubting she could bear a child in her old age.
Is anything too hard for the LORD? At the appointed time he would return, and Sarah would have a son.
Sarah denied laughing, because she was afraid; but he said, no, you did laugh.
The men rose and looked toward Sodom, and Abraham walked with them to send them on their way.
The LORD said, shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?
Abraham was to become a great nation, and all nations of the earth would be blessed through him.
God had chosen him to direct his children to keep the way of the LORD, doing righteousness and justice.
The LORD said the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah was very great, and their sin very grave.
He would go down to see whether their deeds matched the outcry that had reached him.
The men turned toward Sodom, but Abraham remained standing before the LORD.
Abraham drew near and asked whether he would sweep away the righteous with the wicked.
Suppose there were fifty righteous within the city; would he still destroy it rather than spare it for their sake?
Far be it from him to slay the righteous with the wicked; shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?
The LORD said if he found fifty righteous in Sodom, he would spare the whole place for their sake.
Abraham, though only dust and ashes, pressed further, asking about five fewer than fifty.
The LORD agreed he would not destroy it if he found forty-five there.
Abraham asked again about forty; the LORD said he would not do it for the sake of forty.
Abraham asked the LORD not to be angry, and pressed to thirty; the LORD agreed if thirty were found.
Abraham asked about twenty; the LORD said he would not destroy it for twenty's sake.
Abraham asked once more, for ten; the LORD agreed he would not destroy it for ten's sake.
When the LORD finished speaking with Abraham, he departed, and Abraham returned to his place.
